data.title: Is Morningstar (MORN) Pricing Reflects Recent Share Slide And Earnings Multiple Check
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Morningstar is attractively priced right now, it helps to step back and look at what the recent share price and valuation checks are actually telling you.
  • The stock last closed at US$183.21, with returns of 17.9% over the past 30 days, a 3.6% decline over 7 days, and year to date and 1 year returns of 12.9% decline and 35.7% decline respectively, while the 3 year and 5 year returns are 1.7% and 20.4% decline.
  • Recent coverage has focused on Morningstar's role as a data and research provider and how investors may be reassessing its long term position in the investment services space. This context helps frame the recent share price volatility and sets up a closer look at what investors might be paying for that business model today.
  • On our valuation checklist, Morningstar scores 2 out of 6, which means it appears undervalued on some measures but not on others, and next we will walk through those methods before finishing with a smarter way to pull them all together.

Morningstar scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Morningstar Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to earn above the return required by shareholders, then converts that stream of excess earnings into an intrinsic value per share.

For Morningstar, the model starts with a Book Value of $30.75 per share and a Stable EPS of $6.83 per share, based on the median return on equity from the past 5 years. The Average Return on Equity is 17.78%, while the Cost of Equity is $3.12 per share. The difference between what shareholders require and what the business is expected to earn is captured in an Excess Return of $3.71 per share.

The model also uses a Stable Book Value of $38.44 per share, sourced from weighted future Book Value estimates from 2 analysts, to extend these excess returns into the future. Putting this together, the Excess Returns valuation points to an intrinsic value of about $117.13 per share.

Compared with the recent share price of US$183.21, this implies Morningstar is about 56.4% overvalued on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests Morningstar may be overvalued by 56.4%. Discover 48 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Morningstar.

Approach 2: Morningstar Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Morningstar, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand for what the market is paying for each dollar of earnings. This makes it a natural next check alongside the intrinsic value work you saw earlier.

A higher or lower P/E usually reflects what investors expect for future earnings growth and how much risk they see in those earnings. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher “normal” P/E, while lower growth or higher risk tends to justify a lower one.

Morningstar currently trades on a P/E of 19.38x. That sits below the Capital Markets industry average of 22.52x and below the peer group average of 24.74x, which indicates the market is attaching a lower multiple than some comparables.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Morningstar is 14.75x. This is a proprietary view of what P/E might make sense after factoring in the company’s earnings profile, industry, profit margins, size and risk, rather than only lining it up against simple peer or industry averages.

Because the Fair Ratio of 14.75x is below the current P/E of 19.38x, this multiple-based check points to the shares looking overvalued on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED

  • MRI Software LLC joined the Workday partner program to link its facilities management solutions with Workday's financial and human capital management platforms.
  • The combined moves expand Workday's reach in healthcare and facilities management, creating a wider set of workflows for enterprise customers.

Workday, known for its cloud based HR and finance applications, is pushing deeper into sector specific workflows, with healthcare now a clear focus. Fairview Health Services committing to Workday's full AI powered suite, along with integrated supply chain tools, points to demand for unified platforms that can handle workforce, financial, and operational needs in one place.

For investors tracking NasdaqGS:WDAY, the MRI Software partnership and Fairview win highlight fresh use cases across facilities management and healthcare operations. These moves give Workday additional ways to plug into day to day processes, from staffing and budgeting to physical asset management, which could influence how enterprises evaluate end to end software providers over time.

For Workday, Fairview Health Services and MRI Software pull in the same direction: they show how the platform can sit at the center of complex, real world operations rather than just HR and finance back office tasks. Fairview is moving 34,000 employees onto a single, AI-powered system that connects workforce data, financials, and clinical supply flows, which speaks to Workday’s push into regulated, workflow heavy sectors such as healthcare where Oracle and SAP are also active. The MRI partnership extends that idea into the built environment, linking contract bids, staffing, payroll, and asset data in one loop. For you as an investor, the interest lies in Workday’s ability to plug into critical processes that are expensive to rip out once embedded, while still managing the cost and complexity of deeper vertical solutions.

How This Fits Into The Workday Narrative

  • The Fairview and MRI announcements support the narrative that Workday is expanding into new verticals and product lines, using AI-powered workflows and partnerships to widen its role across HR, finance, and operations.
  • Heavier integration work for healthcare and facilities management could reinforce concerns in the narrative about rising investment and regulatory complexity putting pressure on profitability if costs rise faster than revenue.
  • The specific operational data flows from MRI’s facilities tools, and the healthcare supply chain detail at Fairview, are not explicitly spelled out in the narrative, so some of this sector depth may sit outside the current storyline investors are using.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Workday to help decide what it's worth to you.

  • ⚠️ Integrating healthcare and facilities management workflows can increase implementation risk and elongate deployment timelines, which may weigh on near term margins if projects become complex or delayed.
  • ⚠️ As Workday pushes deeper into healthcare and public sector style contracts, regulatory and data privacy requirements could add ongoing compliance costs and raise the bar for product changes.
  • 🎁 Large healthcare clients such as Fairview and ecosystem partners such as MRI can reinforce Workday’s position as a core system of record, potentially improving customer stickiness versus rivals such as Oracle and SAP.
  • 🎁 The flow of operational data back into Workday from MRI’s tools creates scope for richer analytics and AI-driven insights that may strengthen the value of the broader platform for existing and future customers.

On the partnership side, the key test will be whether Workday and MRI can show that integrated bids, staffing, and billing actually simplify customers’ contract execution and reduce manual work.
